prompt:
The Élan case study Section 2 reads as a chain of sentence
fragments. "Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps." Three
sentences of three words each, back to back. It's punchy in
isolation but four paragraphs in a row of this structure becomes
exhausting. Fix the rhythm and tell me when fragment chains earn
their place vs. when they're a tic.
response:
I have everything I need. The picture is complete — CAP-027 is the exact anti-pattern, it's cross-referenced in the Engram dossier and has already burned this section once before (CFB-022/CFB-044). Now I'll deliver the full diagnosis, fix, and the when-to-use doctrine.

---

## Diagnosis

The current Section 2 body is running CAP-027: **Fragment Chain as Default Prose** — the exact anti-pattern that caused the previous full prose rewrite in CFB-022/CFB-044. The issue is structural, not lexical: when three-word declarative sentences repeat four times in sequence, the rhythm stops being a stylistic choice and starts being a nervous tic. The reader doesn't feel punchy energy — they feel the writer running out of air on every breath.

---

## Root Cause — CAP-027

> **CAP-027: Fragment Chain as Default Prose**
> **Status: ACTIVE**
> Category: Voice & Tone

**Trigger:** Three or more consecutive sentences under 8 words, none containing a causal clause ("because," "so," "which meant," "after X, Y"). The fragments are doing *all* the work, and there's nothing longer to give them contrast to land against.

**Why it fails:** Fragments earn their force from what surrounds them. "Not a component library. A control layer." works because it arrives after a full-length sentence that has set up the expectation. Chains of fragments with no full-sentence contrast flatten into telegraph staccato — the rhythm becomes indistinguishable from a shopping list. The dossier already has this recorded: *"Fragment chains are an AI voice tell — section bodies need narrative sentences with cause-and-consequence, not telegraphic lists."*

**Co-pattern — CAP-017 (AI-Generated Voice Tells):** Uniform fragment density across four paragraphs is also a legibility signal that the text was generated, not written. Even if individual sentences are good, the *sameness* of their structure signals machine output.

---

## The Rule: When Fragment Chains Earn Their Place vs. When They're a Tic

This is the doctrine, drawn from voice-style.md §13.1, personal-voice.md T1/T4, and CAP-027.

### ✅ Fragments earn their place when all three are true:

| Condition | What it looks like |
|---|---|
| **1. Arrival, not default** | At least one full sentence (12+ words, subject-verb-object with a causal clause) comes immediately before the fragment chain. The fragment *lands* because the setup gave it somewhere to fall. |
| **2. Maximum two in a row** | After 2 consecutive short fragments, the next sentence must breathe — add a clause, add causation, add a subordinate thought. `personal-voice.md` T1 (Punch-Counterpunch) caps this at 2 before the register must shift. |
| **3. The fragments contain the payoff** | The short sentences carry the conclusion, the reversal, or the gut-punch. The long sentence carries the setup. Never the other way around — don't use fragments as setup and long sentences as payoff, that inverts the emphasis. |

**Canonical example (from personal-voice.md T4 — Escalation Beat):**
> "First time a session produced a correction I'd already made — I wrote it down. Third time it happened, I made it a rule. After forty sessions, the system was checking before it touched a single color."

Three beats, but the third one is a full clause. The fragments land because the third sentence catches them.

### ❌ Fragment chains become a tic when any of these are true:

| Anti-condition | What it sounds like |
|---|---|
| **Four or more paragraphs of the same density** | "Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps. / Added rules. Tested it. Found drift. / Flagged the error. Fixed the route. Iterated again." → The reader stops expecting variation and the rhythm disappears. |
| **Fragments are carrying setup, not punchline** | "The system had 18 skills. Each routed to a domain. Each domain had anti-patterns." → These are setup facts, not payoffs. They want subordinate clauses, not periods. |
| **No sentence in the paragraph exceeds 10 words** | Zero variety = zero contrast = no fragment can land hard. The fragments are all equally weighted. |
| **Zone 3 body text (section body)** | voice-style.md §13.1 caps Zone 3 at 2-3 fragments per section. Fragments are Zone 1 (blurb) register. They leak into Zone 3 as a register mismatch. |

---

## Fix — Section 2 "The System Behind the System"

I don't have the live Payload document, so I need the current text. But based on the dossier, Section 2's argument is: **skills route corrections; anti-patterns encode what not to repeat; the two views (Operations/Knowledge) in SkillMap show the operational and epistemic layers.** The fragment chain problem is almost certainly in the setup sentences — treating enumeration beats as if they're punchlines.

**Paste the current Section 2 body text here** and I'll rewrite every paragraph to spec. But here's the transformation pattern to apply regardless:

### Transformation template

**BEFORE (fragment chain as default):**
```
Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps.
Added rules. Each one explicit. Each one tested.
Eighteen skills. Two hundred patterns. One graph.
```

**AFTER (fragment earns its landing):**
```
The first version had no routing — every correction 
went into one pile, and the agent treated a spacing 
note the same as an architectural decision.

Separating them meant building 18 skills, each 
scoped to a single phase of work. Two hundred 
anti-patterns followed. Not accumulated — designed: 
each one names a specific failure, not a category.
```

The structure: **Long sentence (problem/cause) → medium sentence (action, with subordinate clause) → fragment or short sentence (payoff/verdict).** The rhythm is 3-2-1, not 1-1-1-1.

### Concrete repairs for the four paragraphs

Given the SkillMap section's two views (Operations, Knowledge), here's the paragraph-by-paragraph prescription:

**P1 — The problem** (currently all fragments): Add one sentence that names *why* the system needed two views. The single long sentence becomes the setup; the fragment that ends the paragraph carries the verdict.

```
Before: "Two views. Two purposes. One component."
After:  "Running 18 skills in isolation produces 
         corrections, but it doesn't produce knowledge — 
         you need a second layer that maps what each 
         correction means, not just where it goes. 
         Two views. Same system."
```

**P2 — Operations view**: Don't enumerate the 18 skills with fragment beats. Lead with what the operations view *does*, then let the number land at the end.

```
Before: "18 skills. Sorted by phase. Fully routed."
After:  "The operations view groups each skill by 
         the phase it governs — design, engineering, 
         content, release. The number that matters: 
         18 skills, zero overlap."
```

**P3 — Knowledge view**: The anti-pattern count should be the payoff, not the opener.

```
Before: "200+ patterns. Every domain. All typed."
After:  "The knowledge view is the same data read 
         differently — not what the system does, 
         but what it's learned not to do. 
         200+ anti-patterns, proportional blocks 
         by domain, every edge typed."
```

**P4 — Verdict**: This is the one place a pure fragment chain is earned. P4 *is* the punchline, and you've set it up across three paragraphs now.

```
"Not a library. A memory."
```
Two words. Three words. Lands hard — because you've earned it.

---

## Principle

**voice-style.md §13.1, sentence rules:**
> "Sentences should read like a person writing with conviction — not like a telegram, and not like a press release. If every sentence is under eight words, the rhythm flattens and the voice disappears. Mix short and medium. Let a sentence breathe when the idea needs room."

The long sentence is not the enemy of voice. It is the **weight** that makes the short sentence feel light. Fragment chains are striking because they're surrounded by clauses — strip the clauses out and the fragments have nothing to push against. You're not left with punchiness; you're left with uniform percussion that the eye skips over.

**Drop the current Section 2 body text here and I'll execute the full rewrite against these patterns.**
